text: The video shows the anatomy relevant to a Transabdominal Preperitoneal (TAPP) hernia repair. The median umbilical ligament or urachus, medial umbilical ligament or obliterated umbilical artery, and inferior epigastric vessels forming the lateral umbilical ligament are identified. The left side displays the vas deferens, and the right side shows the testicular vessels, forming the triangle of doom. The indirect inguinal ring is visible, through which the vas deferens vessels traverse. A small cord-like structure, identified as nerves, is present in the lateral triangle of pain.

A finger indicates the starting point for dissection adjacent to the anterior superior iliac spine. The surgeon begins the peritoneal incision near a small tattoo mark, gradually extending it towards the medial side. The incision reveals a direct hernia defect, located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. The peritoneal incision is made at least four centimeters in length.

The surgeon applies traction to open the planes superior to the hernial defect, ensuring the mesh overlap does not interfere with the closure of the peritoneal incision. Dissection is aided by carbon dioxide insufflation, and the surgeon carefully navigates the cobweb spaces to maintain the correct plane. The inferior epigastric vessels are pushed away to prevent injury and bleeding, securing hemostasis for clear visualization. The dissection progresses medially towards the medial umbilical ligament, marking the limit of the peritoneal incision to avoid bladder injury.

The surgeon applies varying traction on the peritoneum to prevent buttonhole injuries, using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to open avascular planes. A small vertical incision is made adjacent to the medial umbilical ligament to access the retropubic space, ensuring the peritoneum does not obstruct the field of vision.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection above the perivascular fat and bladder, maintaining proximity to the avascular space to rapidly open the planes. A hook instrument is employed for comfortable handling, with minimal energy applied to avoid tissue sticking. The pubic symphysis and left pubic arches become visible as dissection progresses, ensuring the bladder remains protected.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral side, encountering the hernia sac of a direct hernia. Small vessels are coagulated using monopolar energy. Dissection below the pubic bone is performed to ensure proper placement of the mesh, staying close to the pubic arch in the avascular space. The right pubic arch and the cupos ligament are clearly exposed, aided by significant traction from the left hand.

Small veins running on the cupos ligament are visible. The surgeon uses bipolar cautery if these veins are in the mesh fixation area. After opening the medial space, attention shifts to the lateral space of Bogro's. Good traction is applied to the peritoneal flap to identify the right plane. The white, thick transversalis fibers are sharply divided, revealing a nicely opening avascular plane. Short bursts of monopolar energy are used, and traction areas are changed to improve exposure. The avascular plane is further dissected and expanded rapidly.

The surgeon reduces the hernia, revealing the white transversalis fascia above the avascular plane and bladder. Using monopolar energy in short bursts, the hernia sac is carefully dissected away. The external iliac vein is visible, requiring careful traction and counter-traction to avoid injury during the retroperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon uses cauterization and applies traction and counter traction to avoid injuring the external iliac vein. The transversus fascia is visible with traction, and the surgeon distinguishes between the fascia and preperitoneal fat to maintain dissection in the correct planes. The bluish peritoneum is thin but not concerning, and traction helps open the planes for mesh placement without unrolling.

The surgeon carefully dissects the chord structures, identifying the peritoneum to avoid creating a buttonhole. The dissection proceeds from the lateral edge of the peritoneum towards the medial side, dividing transversalis fibers with monopolar coagulation. The inferior epigastric artery is visible above the instrument, indicating proximity to the external iliac vein.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to tease the tissues, revealing the external iliac artery to the right of the dissection area. The external iliac vein appears as a bluish hue, and the vas deferens is identified as a white structure. Short bursts of energy are applied to push the testicular vessels away, advancing the parietalization inferiorly to create space for mesh placement. The external iliac vein is carefully visualized, with traction applied to avoid lateral movement and protect the vein.

The surgeon opens the anatomical triangle, revealing the vas deferens and testicular vessels, indicating correct dissection plane. The dissection continues inferiorly to ensure proper mesh placement without rolling. A 15x12 cm mesh is prepared for placement in a TAPP repair, with fixation planned at the cupous ligament and anterior abdominal wall medially. The surgeon assesses the adequacy of dissection and identifies a small attachment between the testicular vessels and peritoneum. Using cautery, the parietalization is advanced, and traction is applied to the peritoneum to enhance space for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to complete the parietalization, carefully avoiding the external iliac vein. The bladder is visible medially, with a thin fascia laterally exposing the obturator space. The obturator vessels and nerve are identified, with the nerve appearing as a whitish structure. Further dissection on the medial side is conducted to ensure adequate space for mesh placement, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels on both sides.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery with the vas deferens passing underneath, marking the endpoint for dissection. A 15x13 cm lightweight macroporous mesh is prepared by rolling two-thirds and securing with a suture for easier handling. The mesh is positioned to ensure a minimum overlap of four centimeters over the defect, extending below the pubic arch and across the pubic symphysis. Using a direct puncture technique, a polypropylene suture is employed to anchor the mesh to the cupous ligament and anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon takes a bite through the cupous ligament with a needle, ensuring it is not a thin fascia. A loose anchoring knot is tied to secure the mesh without tightening excessively, preventing tissue throttling. The surgeon uses a monofilament suture, demonstrating ease of handling. The upper edge of the peritoneum is gently pulled to create more space for mesh placement, allowing the peritoneal flap to hang and facilitate closure.

The surgeon cuts the anchoring suture on the mesh and unrolls it, revealing the mesh's smooth placement due to adequate dissection. The mesh is seen overlapping the midline by approximately two and a half centimeters, ensuring proper coverage. The surgeon then takes a second anchoring suture on the anterior abdominal wall medially.

The surgeon carefully avoids the inferior epigastric vessel to prevent bleeding during the procedure. Hemostasis is secured using compression and bipolar cautery if necessary. A trans-facial suture may be used for persistent bleeding. The surgeon employs a railroading technique to bring the suture through the incision, closing the peritoneum with a monofilament suture, taking multiple bites before pulling the suture together.

The surgeon uses a delayed absorbable polydioxone suture to take thick bites adjacent to the medial umbilical ligament, ensuring the mesh is completely extraperitonealized and not visible. The pneumoperitoneum is maintained at twelve, facilitating the approximation of the peritoneal edges.
